nativeRange.habitat: Channa diplogramma is found in slow-moving and still waters across the Western Ghats of Kerala and Karnataka, India, from low altitudes up to mid-elevation terrain. Natural habitats include forest streams, rivers, swamps, flooded pools, and rice paddies, typically with dense riparian vegetation and leaf litter on the bottom.

decorPreferences: large pieces of driftwood and root tangles
dense emergent and floating plants (water lettuce, Amazon frogbit) for surface cover
leaf litter (Indian almond, oak) to replicate forest-floor biotope
cave-like structures or PVC pipes for retreat

diet.notes: In the wild, the Malabar Snakehead is an obligate piscivore that feeds primarily on fish, frogs, and large invertebrates. In captivity, transitioning the species onto dead or frozen foods — such as tilapia, smelt, whitebait, prawns, and large earthworms — is possible but often takes patience, and live foods are frequently needed at first to trigger a feeding response. Feeder goldfish should be avoided due to disease risk and the presence of thiaminase, a compound that interferes with vitamin B1 absorption. Adult fish generally do well on two to three feedings per week; juveniles typically require four to five. Flake and pellet foods are almost universally refused.

goodWith: large, robust bottom-dwellers that occupy different zones (large plecos, large doradid catfish) — with caution and in very large tanks only
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be eaten
conspecifics (highly territorial; severe fighting between adults)
medium-sized cichlids or barbs (will predate or be harassed)

imageCandidates.notes: Anglertom, 4208x2368. Live wild adult held riverside in situ; sharp, whole fish in frame, shows the dark lateral blotching diagnostic of the species. Colour is the darker wild tone rather than captive reddish-orange, but best available live photo.
shakalaka, 620x340. Live specimen in an aquarium; in focus, whole fish shown, pale body with dark lateral blotching. Lower resolution, so kept as secondary.

summary: The Malabar Snakehead (Channa diplogramma) is a large piscivore from India’s Western Ghats, recognized as a distinct species in 2011. Adults reach up to 65 cm and are IUCN Vulnerable. All Channa species are banned from US import and interstate transport. Best suited to experienced keepers with a species-only tank of 600 L or more.
careNotes: Channa diplogramma was long treated as a synonym of the Giant Snakehead (C. micropeltes), but a 2011 taxonomic revision recognized it as a morphologically and molecularly distinct species native to the Western Ghats of Kerala and Karnataka, India. Adults develop a striking reddish-orange body with dark lateral blotching — a key visual distinction from the more widely distributed C. micropeltes. Maximum published total length is approximately 65 cm, though most captive specimens reach 50–60 cm. As a labyrinth-adjacent obligate air-breather, C. diplogramma must have unobstructed access to the water surface at all times. A tight-fitting, weighted lid is non-negotiable — this species is a powerful, unpredictable jumper and will escape through any gap. A minimum 600 L tank with a footprint of at least 180 cm is recommended for a single adult, with robust external filtration (the fish is a heavy feeder and produces significant waste). Water temperature should be maintained at 22–28°C; pH 6.0–7.5 suits the soft, slightly acidic Western Ghats streams of its origin. Water changes of 30–40% weekly are essential to manage ammonia and nitrate. This species is best maintained as a solitary specimen; conspecific pairs are extremely difficult and require very large tanks with visual barriers — do not attempt without expert-level experience. In the US, all Channa species are listed as Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act (18 U.S.C. § 42); import and interstate transport are federally prohibited. Check local state law for additional restrictions.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Like other large snakeheads, C. diplogramma is presumed to be an open/substrate spawner with biparental guarding of eggs and early fry. Conditioning requires a spacious, heavily planted dedicated breeding tank, temperature increase to ~28°C, and intensive live-food feeding over several weeks. No reliable captive breeding protocols have been documented in the hobbyist literature as of 2026. Brood sizes in closely related large Channa species range from several hundred to over a thousand eggs.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Channa diplogramma
primary.notes: Primary source. Provides morphological description, native range (Western Ghats), water parameters, tank size recommendations, diet, and husbandry notes. Confirms resurrection from synonymy with C. micropeltes by Britz et al. 2011.

additional.name: FishBase — Channa diplogramma
additional.notes: Cross-referenced adult size (to 65 cm TL), taxonomy (Channidae, Anabantiformes), native range (India: Kerala, Karnataka), and IUCN Vulnerable status. Confirms species description by Day 1865 and resurrection by Britz et al. 2011.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Channa diplogramma
additional.notes: Confirms IUCN Vulnerable listing for C. diplogramma; cites restricted endemic range in the Western Ghats biodiversity hotspot and habitat loss pressures.

additional.name: USDA APHIS — Federal Injurious Wildlife: Snakeheads (Channidae)
additional.notes: Confirms all Channa spp. listed as Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act; import and interstate transport prohibited in the US without a federal permit.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Snakehead species guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist-facing overview of large Channa species including C. diplogramma; notes adult colouration, large tank requirements, piscivore diet, and escape risk.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding protocols documented. Presumed open/substrate spawner with biparental guarding; conditioning requires large dedicated tank, elevated temperature, and intensive live feeding.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: All Channa species are USDA-listed Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act; import and interstate transport banned in the US. Reaches 60+cm. Western Ghats endemic; IUCN Vulnerable.
